# Cell Structure
____
## Types of cells and their organelles

### Prokaryotic
**![109](data:image/png;base64,)**
- no nucleus
    
- no membrane-bound organelles
    
- always unicellular
    
- only found in kingdom Monera (bacteria group)
#### Organelles:
- plasma membrane (selective double layer of phospholipids)
    
- cell wall (barrier that shapes and protects)
    
- ribosomes (host organelle for protein synthesis)
    
- nucleoid (the genetic material in the cell)
### Eukaryotic (which is actually more relevant)

**![207](data:image/png;base64,)**
- has a nucleus

    
- has membrane-bound organelles
    
- can be both unicellular and multicellular
    
- found in animal and plant cells
#### Organelles

- Nucleus: site of replication, transcription and posttranscriptional modification of RNA; cell’s control center; 
    
- Nucleolus: produces and assembles cell’s ribosomes
    
- Nuclear pores: channel in the nuclear envelope that regulates the transportation of molecules between the nucleus and the cytoplasm
    
- Ribosomes: synthesize proteins by translating mRNA into an amino acid sequence
    
- Golgi complex: processing and packaging proteins that have exited the rough endoplasmic reticulum to be further transported inside and/or outside the cell. In plant cells, the Golgi body also serves as the site for the synthesis of complex polysaccharides; post office of the cell
    
- Rough endoplasmic reticulum: Folding of proteins
    
- Smooth endoplasmic reticulum: Creates and stores lipids
    
- Mitochondria: Powerhouse of the cell
    
- Vacuole: storage structure; can hold food or waste in a cell;
    
- Lysosome: digestive system of the cell (breaks down lipids, carbohydrates and proteins)
    
- Peroxisomes: carry out oxidative reactions using molecular oxygen and generate hydrogen peroxide;
    
- Chloroplast: produce energy through photosynthesis
    
- Cytoskeleton: provides structure to the cell

## The cell membrane

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**

The cell membrane is a **selectively permeable boundary** that separates the cell's internal environment from the external environment.

It controls:

- What enters the cell
	
- What leaves the cell
	
- How the cell communicates
	
- How electrical signals are generated

For neuroscience, the membrane is the foundation of neural computation.

### Components of mosaic

- Phospholipids
    
* Cholesterol: helps membrane to remain fluid and avoid freezing
    
- Integral proteins: integrated into the membrane
    
- Peripheral proteins: in periphery of the membrane 
    
- Carbohydrates: attached to either lipids or proteins

### Lipid bilayer

 - Two layers of phospholipids
	
- Heads face water, tails face inward
	
- Creates a hydrophobic barrier
	
* Acts as a selectively permeable membrane: small nonpolar molecules can pass easily, bigger components need transport proteins

**![419](data:image/png;base64,)**

### Membrane proteins

Membrane proteins can be peripheral (on "top" of the membrane, not going through both sides) or integral (built in and integrated in the membrane). Types include (but are not only):
#### Ion channels

- Pores that let specific ions pass (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺)
	
- Can be open/closed (gated)
	
* Types are:
	* Voltage-gated (open depending on membrane voltage)
	* Ligand-gated (open when a certain chemical binds)
	* Leak channels (always partially open)
#### Receptors

They basically detect molecules and trigger internal changes
* Ionotropic receotors
	
	* Ligand-gated ion channels, fast synaptic transmission
	
* Metabotropic receptors
	
	* Works via G-proteins (more info in cell signaling section) and signaling cascades and functions as signal amplifier, excitability modulator, long-term state changer
